topic.name: Lewis and Clark Expedition
topic.description: The Corps of Discovery expedition (1804-1806) led by Meriwether Lewis and William Clark to explore the newly acquired Louisiana Territory and find a route to the Pacific Ocean.

events.title: Jefferson Commissions the Expedition
events.description: President Thomas Jefferson asked Congress to fund a secret expedition to explore the Missouri River and find a route to the Pacific. Congress approved $2,500 for the mission.
events.significance: Jefferson's vision of westward expansion launched one of the most important scientific and geographic explorations in American history.

events.title: Louisiana Purchase
events.description: The United States purchased approximately 828,000 square miles of territory from France for $15 million, doubling the size of the nation and providing the primary territory for the expedition to explore.
events.significance: The Louisiana Purchase dramatically expanded the United States and made the Lewis and Clark expedition essential for understanding and claiming the new territory.

events.title: Corps of Discovery Departs Camp Dubois
events.description: The Corps of Discovery, consisting of about 45 men, set out from Camp Dubois near present-day Hartford, Illinois, beginning their journey up the Missouri River.
events.significance: The official start of the expedition launched the most ambitious exploration of the North American continent undertaken by the United States government.

events.title: Sacagawea Joins the Expedition
events.description: The Corps hired French-Canadian fur trader Toussaint Charbonneau as an interpreter. His Shoshone wife Sacagawea, who had been captured as a child, joined the expedition as a guide and interpreter.
events.significance: Sacagawea's knowledge of the terrain, native languages, and her presence as a woman with an infant signaled peaceful intentions to tribes the Corps encountered.

events.title: Winter at Fort Mandan
events.description: The Corps built Fort Mandan near present-day Washburn, North Dakota, and spent the winter among the Mandan and Hidatsa peoples. Lewis and Clark gathered crucial geographic intelligence from the tribes about the lands ahead.
events.significance: The winter camp allowed the Corps to survive brutal conditions, gather intelligence, and send specimens and reports back to President Jefferson.

events.title: Portage Around the Great Falls
events.description: The Corps discovered the Great Falls of the Missouri River in present-day Montana. What Lewis initially estimated as a half-day portage took 18 days as the men hauled heavy canoes and supplies around a series of five waterfalls.
events.significance: The grueling portage tested the Corps' endurance and demonstrated the immense physical challenges of the journey westward.

events.title: Corps Crosses the Continental Divide
events.description: The Corps crossed the Continental Divide at Lemhi Pass on the present-day Montana-Idaho border. From the summit, Lewis discovered that the Pacific slope was not a short portage but a vast mountain range.
events.significance: Crossing the Continental Divide revealed that Jefferson's hope for a short water route to the Pacific was impossible, reshaping understanding of western North American geography.

events.title: Crossing the Bitterroot Mountains
events.description: The Corps endured one of the most harrowing legs of the journey crossing the Bitterroot Mountains (Lolo Trail) in present-day Idaho. Snow, cold, and scarce game nearly destroyed the expedition.
events.significance: The treacherous mountain crossing pushed the Corps to the edge of survival and stands as one of the most physically demanding achievements of the expedition.

events.title: Corps Reaches the Pacific Ocean
events.description: The Corps of Discovery reached the Pacific Ocean near present-day Astoria, Oregon. Clark recorded in his journal: 'Ocian in view! O! the joy.' The entire Corps, including Sacagawea and York, voted on where to spend the winter.
events.significance: Reaching the Pacific fulfilled the expedition's primary goal and established an American claim to the Oregon Territory. The democratic vote including Sacagawea and York was unprecedented.

events.title: Winter at Fort Clatsop
events.description: The Corps built Fort Clatsop near present-day Astoria, Oregon, and spent a wet, miserable winter. Lewis and Clark used the time to write detailed journals documenting flora, fauna, geography, and Native peoples encountered on the journey.
events.significance: The journals and scientific observations compiled at Fort Clatsop became invaluable records of western North America and its indigenous peoples.

events.title: Corps Returns to St. Louis
events.description: The Corps of Discovery returned to St. Louis to a hero's welcome after traveling over 8,000 miles in two years, four months, and ten days. All but one member of the party survived the journey.
events.significance: The successful return of the Corps provided the United States with vast geographic, scientific, and ethnographic knowledge of the West and strengthened American claims to the continent.
